title_tesim: Page of article regarding the one hundredth anniversary of the Pony Express postal service, 1960-1964
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: B. Herrick - John Crum. Spur.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph copy of an article page regarding the 100th anniversary of the Pony Express postal service. The article focuses on Alexander Majors, a Methodist lay preacher and Western wagon freight boss, who founded the Pony Express along with partners William H. Russel and William B. Waddell. The article also notes the sale of commemorative postage stamps celebrating the first ride from St. Joseph, Missouri to Sacramento, California beginning July 19 (1960). An illustration and photo with captions precede the text. At top, an illustration of a man wearing western attire riding on horseback. He is being pursued by Native Americans (also on horseback). The accompanying caption reads: "Hostile Indians were a constant hazard for riders - as caught here by the Western artist, Herman W. Hansen." Below left, a portrait of Alexander Majors, one of the Pony Express founders. Caption reads: "A. Majors: Lay preacher best remembered for his experiment that failed." Remaining text reads: "April, 1960, Marks the 100th Anniversary of the Pony Express from...St. Joe to Sacramento."

Negative sleeve information does not match photograph. This reprinted article matches one housed in another negative sleeve. Text from negative sleeve: Pony Express, 1860-1960. 1964 ME Church Magazine, Chicago. (For associated image, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d7f10.)
A date of 1964 is noted on the negative sleeve. It is possibly a reprint date. Text within the article suggest it was written and published in 1960.

title_tesim: Captain Baksey, (Santa Monica?), 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Capt. Baksey. 20th St. c/o John Crum. 4/23/42.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Nautical-themed photograph of Captain Baksey, smoking a pipe and wearing a fisherman's cap, pull over shirt and pants. He is holding a length of boating rope in both hands. He stands against a dwelling with clapboard siding. Hanging from the wall behind him is a lifebuoy with words painted around the ring, "A Caine" being the only visible text.

"A Caine" ("[...]NIE A Caine" in other photos) probably refers to the ship "Minnie A Caine," a four-masted schooner that was built in Seattle in 1900 by Moran Bros. for Charles Nelson of San Francisco. The ship was blown ashore north of Santa Monica in 1939 and was burned for her metal the same year.
Negative sleeve notes John Crum, a resident of Santa Monica. A number of Crum's memorabilia items were photographed by Adelbert Bartlett and are represented in this collection.

title_tesim: Article page regarding a Northern Pacific locomotive
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Old UP Locomotive.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph copy of an article page titled, "The train that brought 5 states into the Union." The page has been photographed or cropped to capture the image on the top half of the page. The image is of a locomotive bedecked with garlands and American flags. A sign posted on the front of the locomotive reads: "N.P.R.R. St. Paul Minnesota to Portland Oregon." Several men wearing late 1890s attire stand on and next to the train, while roughly a dozen men and boys are viewed standing on the platform. Most look toward the camera.

title_tesim: James B. Lockwood with local child, Santa Monica, 1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: From Adelbert Bartlett, 1572 West Pico, Los Angeles. 2 negs. 2635 P1 to 3. James B. Lockwood, Santa Monica, Calif. Served as pitcher and second baseman with orig. Chicago Cubs forty years ago under Capt. Anson. Now a gardener and as enthusiastic about it as he is of baseball. His son will shortly become a professional baseball player with Angels of Pacific Coast League. Lockwood is shown in pictures with a group of his young friends with whom he frequently plays on near-by sand lot when he is not attending to his garden.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 265 P2 Bartlett L A
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d77mw (document)
description_tesim: Photograph of James B. Lockwood, arm raised and gripping a baseball, posed with a local boy seen holding a bat. Print on the bat reads: "Hanna Batrite Reg. U.S. Pat. Off. Athens, Ga... Santa Monica Schools...Hickory Driver WTA Ryan 28 Super Tough." Photographed in front of Lockwood's garden and home, 1444 Yale St., Santa Monica.
Related typescript document further describes Lockwood and provides photograph date (see "Relation" note).
The name James B. Lockwood does not appear on any roster for the Chicago Cubs (1902-present), Chicago Orphans (1898-1901), Chicago Colts (1894-1897) or Chicago White Stockings (1876-1893).

title_tesim: Santa Monica's host city float in the California Admission Day Parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
named_subject_tesim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

This float appears to have had two reproductions of the California State Seal with two living representations of “Minerva” seated on opposite sides. For alternate views of this float, see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7fzz and 21198/zz002d7g3h.

Photograph of Santa Monica’s host city float in the California Admission Day parade. Spectators are viewed in front of Security First National Bank at 4th St. and Santa Monica Blvd, watching the float pass by. At the center of the float is a reproduction of the Seal of California, including a live model posed as the Roman goddess Minerva, seated and holding a staff with a shield propped near her feet. The seal’s other symbolic emblems – a California grizzly bear feeding on a cluster of grapes, a sheaf of wheat, a miner with pickax, and ships sailing in the bay – are all represented here. The phrase “Eureka” is printed below thirty-one stars, above the mountains and rising sun. Surrounding the image are the words “The Great Seal of the State of California,” replicating the version of the state seal that was standardized in 1937. To the left of the shield is a Spanish conquistador (probably Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo). Smaller statues of Franciscan monks are placed on a ledge below a small model ship on the float’s platform. At right is a contemporary view of the Santa Monica skyline surrounded by foliage. Words along the side of the platform read: “Santa Monica – California's Host 1937.”

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West

Photograph of a float submitted by the city of San Francisco, appearing in the California Admission Day parade. Two women are viewed standing amidst the floral display – one wearing a Spanish-style dress, a mantilla, and a beaded necklace with a large cross; the other wears a silk or satin robe with long kimono sleeves and what appears to be a flower in her hair. Spelled out in flowers along two sides of the float are the words: “World’s 1939 Fair…San Francisco Invites You. N.D.G.W…N.S.G.W.” Banners hang from lines attached to utility poles, with one at center reading: “Welcome, Native Sons & Daughters of the Golden West.” The parade route follows Ocean Ave., with the Dinmar Apartments viewed in the background (at the corner of Ocean and Santa Monica Blvd.).

description_tesim: Photograph view facing north toward the Santa Monica Bay and lighthouse building on Will Rogers State Beach, taken atop a Palisades cliff. A portion of Pacific Coast Highway is seen bottom left; a cactus grows with shrubbery on the cliffs at right.
The lighthouse building was purchased by Will Rogers in 1928 and eventually demolished in 1978. It was not a functioning lighthouse. Co-ordinates derived from original lighthouse location.

One of a series of photographs labeled 2890 P 1-5. All were shot from the same cliff toward the lighthouse building. Images labeled “2890 P 1-3” look to be from the 1930s. The building displays minimal signage and 1930s era automobiles are in view. Images labeled “2890 P 4-5” include 1940s era cars, while the building itself has accumulated more signage and is most similar to an additional photograph (No. 2902) of Caroline Bartlett taken at the same location, dated 1946.

The Lofty and Exalted Order of Uplifters was a men's social club founded by Harry Marston Haldeman in 1913. The group operated out of the Los Angeles Athletic Club until the purchase of "Uplifters Ranch" in 1920. The ranch land and associated clubhouse (currently the Rustic Canyon Recreation Center) are located in Rustic Canyon - a neighborhood of Pacific Palisades.

description_tesim: The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

Photograph of a boy seated at a school desk in front of a fireplace playing a toy drum at the Children's Home Society. Carousel horses are painted on the side of the drum. Two socks on the fireplace indicate that it is Christmas time.

title_tesim: All female marching band and Ladies' Auxiliary groups in the California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2875. Veterans Parade, Santa Monica, 1938.
named_subject_tesim: Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. The event was noted in the article, “All but Two Ride in Parade: Pair of Nonagenarian G.A.R. Veterans Still Able to March Afoot,” Los Angeles Times, 5 May 1938: A2

Photograph of an all female marching band and Ladies' Auxiliary groups  marching in the annual parade of the California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic. They are lead by a five member band - two playing wood-wind instruments, three playing drums and bass drum. They wear dark military caps embroidered with "OUV," dark colored jackets and white skirts. They are followed by women wearing white military caps, blouses with ties, vests, and skirts. Additional women's groups proceed behind them. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured nearing 3rd Street. Automobiles are parked along the commercial thoroughfare in front of a two-story building with a variety of stores on the ground floor. Signs on the building and printed on awnings read: "Medical Building," “[The D.B Wright Invest]ment… [Real Estate Insurance] Rentals… 321...Estate,” “Marinello” “Health Foods... 325 Santa Monica Health Food Co.,” “Medical Building 327; “…Shoppe” and “Templeman’s.” An additional store front (name illegible) sits next to a corner space drug store. Farther down the street, a Texaco gas station and its sign are viewed next to the Leon Apartments.
combined_subject_ssim: Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California

description_tesim: The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. The event was noted in the article, “All but Two Ride in Parade: Pair of Nonagenarian G.A.R. Veterans Still Able to March Afoot,” Los Angeles Times, 5 May 1938: A2

Photograph of a Shriners drum corps in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. The drummers wear light-colored shirts and pants with sashes around the waist, bow ties and Shriner's hats. The men are led by a drum major wearing a suit and tie. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured here at Third Street. Automobiles are parked along the commercial thoroughfare in front of a variety of businesses. One auto advertises a business on the driver's side door. Text reads: "Bay Cities Electic Co., 1436 4th St. Arcade, Phone 21288 [or 0]." Signs on the buildings and printed on awnings read: “Yardage, Woolen,” “Maher Music Co.,” “Wilshire Cafeteria,” “Pan Pan Studios, Honey Bell Gifts,  “The D.B Wright Investment Co.," “Marinello” and “Health Foods.”

description_tesim: Camp Fire Girls of America, founded in 1910, was the first secular, multicultural organization for girls in the United States.

The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. The event was noted in the article, “All but Two Ride in Parade: Pair of Nonagenarian G.A.R. Veterans Still Able to March Afoot,” Los Angeles Times, 5 May 1938: A2

Photograph of a Camp Fire Girls troop in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. The girls wear uniforms with neck scarves. The organization's emblem of two crossed wood logs and a flame is viewed on their right-hand pockets. Behind them stands a majorette followed by a marching band. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured here at 3rd Street. Automobiles are parked along the commercial thoroughfare in front of a variety of businesses. Signs on the buildings and printed on awnings read: “Ernest St… Optometrist,” "Maher Music Co.” and “[Wilshire] Cafeteria.” The Leon Apartments are viewed at the end of the block.

description_tesim: Related to the Los Angeles Times article, "Operetta Staged in Santa Monica," 21 Jan. 1952: A9.
This production of "The Student Prince" was presented by the Phillips Foundation under the auspices of the Culver City Light Opera Association.

Group portrait of the cast of "The Student Prince" in costume on stage at Barnum Hall with the orchestra pit in the foreground. Among the case are, in the front row: Donna Phillips (Princess Margaret, 5th from left), George Tule (Prince Karl Franz, 7th from left), Shirley O. Mills (Kathy, 8th from left), Irma Caron (Grand Duchess Anastasia, 10th from left), Bobbie King (Countess Leyden, 11th from left), Paul Keast (Dr. Engel, 12 from left) and Thomas Glynn (Herr Ruder, 3rd from right).

title_tesim: Santa Monica Elks Lodge No. 906 float entry and participants, Will Rogers Memorial Celebration parade, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2901

Text from negative sleeve: 2901. Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. July 27/1940 in Santa Monica. Street Parade. Elks float. "Death Valley" Scotty. Mayor Claude Crawford, -- Johnson, Scotty's partner in group photo; Don Jackson, Carolyn Bartlett. 3: Dr Claude C Crawford U.S.C., Earl T Crawford, Barker Bros, Claude Crawford, mayor of Santa Monica.
named_subject_tesim: Elks (Fraternal order).  Lodge No. 906 (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a Will Rogers themed float, entered by the Santa Monica Elks lodge #906 into the "Roundup of the Stars" parade - part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. A car sits at foreground carrying four young in white. A sign hanging on the car door reads: "Officers, Santa Monica Lodge, B.P.O. Elks #906 and William N. Smith, Marshall, Elks Will Rogers Essay Contest." Outside of the car stands a man wearing a white suit with dark tie and pocket square. Several Elks members (wearing loose sleeve shirts with sashes at the waists and wearing fezzes) are also viewed standing around the car. Their entry float includes an image of Rogers on a clock face. Above reads, "To Our Absent Brother." Santa Monica City Hall can be viewed beyond the float in the background.

Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940: A1.

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.
combined_subject_ssim: Elks (Fraternal order).  Lodge No. 906 (Santa Monica, Calif.)

Photograph of three actresses, dressed in western/cowgirl attire, seated on horseback at the "Roundup of the Stars" parade - part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. From left to right: Evelyn Venable, Ann Rutherford, and Linda Ware. The actresses wear cowboy hats, vests, neckerchiefs, skirts and boots, and wave to the crowds out of view. The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

Photograph of a actress Joyce Reynolds (possibly) riding along the "Roundup of the Stars" parade route, part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. She wears a fur coat and fascinator headpiece, and is seen waving from the car - seated on the convertible's top boot. Spectators are viewed along the parade route that passed through business and residential streets. Here, what appears to be a residence is seen in the background at right. In front of the structure is a sign advertising "Hat Works" (smaller font is illegible).

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

Photograph of actress Linda Ware, dressed in western/cowgirl attire and seated on horseback at the "Roundup of the Stars" parade, part of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. Ware's ensemble is comprised of a cowboy hat, fringed vest and skirt, leather wrist cuffs and cowboy boots. She wears a sash that reads, "Miss Highway 66." Spectators are seated and standing along the parade route that followed along Palisades Park (the original rustic branch fence is viewed in the background beyond the palm trees).

title_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story "Ogwalla Springs," by Adelbert Bartlett and Mrs. Williams, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761, "Ogwallah Springs", desert story illustrations, 9/1/42. By Mrs. Williams & Adelbert Bartlett.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story "Ogwalla Springs," written by Adelbert Bartlett, possibly illustrated by Mrs. Williams. Here, the illustrations flow across two pages. At left, a man wearing a sombrero, vest and full-sleeve shirt plays a guitar and serenades a young woman wearing a cowboy hat, plaid shirt, and neckerchief. The name "Maisie Lou" is written at right. Above the troubadour are treble clef notes surrounding his song's lyric: "My gal up in them thar pink clouds." A rope border runs vertically along their section, then loops around a revolver and a horse saddle. An image of a horse bucking its rider (who exclaims "YIPPEE!") continues the action to the right side page, culminating in a partial view of a horse with a thought bubble over its head (most of the text has been cropped out of the frame). In addition, for sections have been left empty for printed text. A number "4" - likely the page number- is circled at the bottom of the left hand page. What appears to be "KW" is rendered next to the female.
Related image includes caption: "illustrations by W. Williams" (image ark no. 21198/zz002d89nt).

title_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story "Ogwalla Springs," by Adelbert Bartlett and Mrs. Williams, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761, "Ogwallah Springs", desert story illustrations, 9/1/42. By Mrs. Williams & Adelbert Bartlett.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story, "Ogwalla Springs," by Adelbert Bartlett and Mrs. Williams, possibly drawn by Mrs. Williams (no full name or initials on illustration). Illustration carries across both pages. Page at right depicts the name "Oqwalla" written above a sun, rendered with a human face that winks at the viewer, its rays shining over a man resting on his back, wearing a cowboy hat and pair of patterned shorts, and smoking a pipe. Drawn in the left-hand corner is a woman reading a book. Only her head and hands are visible. To the right, a man sits under a palm tree with his hat pulled down over his eyes. An animal skull rests near him. Beyond them sits the town of Ogwalla with built structures, two tipis, and fences depicted. The number "5" is circled at the bottom of the page, likely indicating page number.
Related image includes caption: "illustrations by W. Williams" (image ark no. 21198/zz002d89nt).

title_tesim: Assembly performance at the Memorial Greek Ampitheatre, Santa Monica, 1937-1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SMHi Art Rm. Open House Nite. [Sleeve is an envelope addressed to Mr. Adelbert Bartlett with postal cancelation: "Los Angeles, Calif., Apr 25, 1937."]
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of a performance at the Memorial Greek Ampitheatre on the Santa Monica High School campus. One male, seated at a desk, watches as three performers huddle together in conversation. A fourth performer is seen either sitting or rising from one of two chairs placed at center. Behind the sparse stage set is a stand holding a six flags from various countries. Student spectators are viewed at photo foreground. Musicians with their instruments and sheet music are seen in the first few rows nearest the pit. Performance photographed by Carolyn Bartlett.

Photographs housed with this negative sleeve are related to others from a sleeve labeled "Carolyn's negatives." The photographs are visually and thematically similar. In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of Santa Monica High School structures built between 1937 and 1939 by the Works Project Administration (WPA). Both sleeves also included construction site images, likely related to the WPA’s reconstruction project (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8j8h and 198/zz002d8k9h).
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

Photograph of two women standing in front of a display case housing Mexican folk objects. The objects are on display for the art department's open house night.  Objects on display include a serape blanket, a sombrero, and a figurine of a man riding a horse or donkey. Hanging at left is a painting of a mariachi playing a guitar, the words "La Fiesta" printed over the image. Photograph probably taken by Carolyn Bartlett, Santa Monica High School.

description_tesim: Photograph of a student placing a Union Flag on a stand with seven other flags. The stand is set up on the grassy stage of the Open-Air Memorial Theater on the Santa Monica High School campus.

Handwritten list of participants in the Children’s Floral Parade. Clockwise from top left: “Ventura Mgette [Majorette], Bruce Henderson, Director. A note referencing Ventura Majorettes possibly refers to image ark no. 21198/zz002d8nj3.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by Santa Monica club women and civic leaders, with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

An additional image shows a two-story building linked to a similar one-story structure and covered walkways. That photo was taken in 1939 (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d5j01).
Portions of Santa Monica High School were destroyed during the earthquake of 1933. Its reconstruction fell under the Works Project Administration (WPA). The project was completed in 1939.

description_tesim: Handwritten list of participants in the Children’s Floral Parade. Transcription from left side: “Bike, Joan Noble, Ger dir[?] Reynolds. La Von Merrill, Mrs Belle, 650 Flower Ave., Peace World." [Right side:] Fancy Dresser up little girl. Mrs JH Williams, Anne Marie Williams, 3072 Pacific Ave., Venice.”

Notation regarding two women (Noble and Reynolds) probably relates to two images featuring women with bikes. In addition, three photographs feature a young girl in "fancy" dress, possibly Anne Marie Williams. Finally, the notation referencing La Von Merrill and Mrs. Belle probably relates to an image featuring a young girl on a "World Peace" float. Numbers arranged respectively (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8nb0, 21198/zz002d8nm4, 21198/zz002d8ng2, 21198/zz002d8nnn, 21198/zz002d8nxs, and 21198/zz002d8nch).

title_tesim: Girls singing from a song book at the Children's Home Society, Los Angeles, 1935-1960
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2593. Children's Home L.A.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Photograph of three young girls, wearing coats and beanies, singing from a song book at the Children's Home Society. They are seated on a piano bench.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

title_tesim: Nelda Scarsella, Gene Curtsinger and Enrico Porta in “La Traviata” at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 20
Text from negative sleeve: Frank Pace. Opera Traviata, 9/15/51.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 3. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta. She is seated on a settee next to Gene Curtsinger (in the role of Alfredo Germont). Enrico Porta, portraying Giorgio Germont, stands at right, while unidentified performers portraying Dr. Grenvil and Annina (Violetta’s maid) stand in the background. The setting is Violetta’s bedroom in her Parisian home. Props include a bed, lace covered nightstand, side table, chairs and a settee. Beyond the stage, musicians can be seen in shadow within the orchestra pit at foreground.

title_tesim: Opera singer Nelda Scarsella performing in “La Traviata” at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 10
Text from negative sleeve: Frank Pace. Opera Traviata, 9/15/51.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 1. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta. She stands alone, center stage, wearing a tiered gown with lace trim. The setting is a room in Violetta’s Parisian home. The set’s design and props reflect an opulent space, including upholstered chairs, a settee, and a chandelier hanging above a table set with a lace table cloth, candelabra, plates, and champagne goblets. Beyond the stage in the orchestra pit, a musician is viewed in shadow playing a double bass.

Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

title_tesim: International Sourdough Reunion banquet, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 17
Text from negative sleeve: 2907. Yukon Society Convention 1949. Wm H Brown L.A.
named_subject_tesim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Men and women wearing suits and ties or dresses, photographed while seated at tables set for dining. The men and women are attending an International Sourdough Reunion banquet, hosted by the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles.

International Sourdough Reunion, Inc. is a non-profit association of fraternal organizations that share the pioneer history of Alaska and the Yukon in common. Each year, one organization hosts a convention of members from groups located as far north as Fairbanks, Alaska, and as far south as San Diego, California (with points in between). In 1949, the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California hosted the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion convention. Events were held at the Hotel Alexandria in Los Angeles. The four-day event (August 17-20) was noted in the article, "Gold Rush is On: Tales of '98 Relived at Sourdough Meeting," Los Angeles Times, 18 Aug. 1949: A2.
combined_subject_ssim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California

title_tesim: Photograph of 2nd page of Los Angeles Times Sunday Magazine story about Drum Barracks, "When Camels Carried Mail to California," by Basil Heathcote, 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1823 - 1824 253R1694 2 negs, 1929.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Drum Barracks, 1862-68, offcers qtrs. U.S. Army, Wilmington (now in Los Angeles at L.A. Harbor) Calif. (see story attached)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Text reads, in part: Mr. Keaveny, who has made a study of early California history for the past twenty years, has spared no effort to preserve and restore what remains of old Drum Barracks.  There are fourteen rooms ... Early in 1860 and through 1861 an army post was maintained in San Pedro ... housed in tents ... Gen. Phineas Banning, whose enterprise and foresight had built the town of Wilmington, offered an adequate tract of land here ... Drum Barracks was there constructed in 1862 and named in honor of the Adjutant-General of the Army, Gen. Drum ... Here was the scene of the first and only government experiment with camels. ... When Jefferson Davis was Secretary of War in the Cabinet of President Pierce, he ordered the purchase of seventy-five camels and dromedaries from Egypt and Arabia to be used to transport munitions and supplies ... The camels ... were finally sold to the highest bidders at Drum Barracks at the close of the Civil War. ...
Includes photograhs of General Phineas Banning and Drum Barracks officers' quarters and jail building

title_tesim: Photograph of 1st page of Los Angeles Times Sunday Magazine story about Drum Barracks, "When Camels Carried Mail to California," by Basil Heathcote, 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1823 - 1824 253R1694 2 negs, 1929.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Drum Barracks, 1862-68, offcers qtrs. U.S. Army, Wilmington (now in Los Angeles at L.A. Harbor) Calif. (see story attached)
Handwritten in pencil at upper right: 1823
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Text reads, in part: When the camel cavalcade carried the soldiers' mail and provisions to New Mexico, Arizona and California, old Drum Barracks, then Headquarters Military Base for the Armies of the Southwest, was in the first importance of its youth.  Its imposing group of buildings dominated the awakening village of Wilmington ... To walk through the rooms of this old landmark today ... is to hear the echoes of Drum Barracks of 1862.  ... Various were the fates of these historic buildings.  The soldiers' barracks accidentally burned down. ... One fine old structure is left--the officers' quarters, now the home of Mr. and Mrs. Thomas Keaveny. ... the imposing white house stands almost as it did in the early days. ...

Includes colored drawing of camels, soldiers, rocks, and joshua trees, with caption: Romance Still Clings to Old Drum Barracks, Headquarters in 1862 for Uncle Sam's Picturesque Experiment With the "Dromedary Express."  "Drum Barracks" is circled in pencil.

description_tesim: The Hotel Playa Ensenada opened on October 31, 1930. Later known as the Hotel Riviera del Pacifico, it was an elegant gambling casino developed by the North American Compañía Mexicana del Rosarito. The architect was Gordon E. Mayer. The hotel was closed and partly demolished in 1964. Partly rebuilt in 1978, the hotel was reopened as the Centro Social, Cívico y Cultural de Ensenada

title_tesim: Typewritten caption for photograph(s?) of Mawby triplets at Lake Arrowhead, [1928?]
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 700-to-869 series, Mawby Triplets, Malibu, 1928.  [In faint pencil, possibly erased:] Edit out at Printing, John Daniels

Text reads: Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15h Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  845 to 848.  TRIPLET SNOW BABIES AND A SNOW MAN.  Claudine, Claudette and Angella Mawby, 7-year-old English triplets from London, who make their home in California, are shown enjoying their very first real snow man at mile-high Lake Arrowhead in the San Bernardino mountains above the palms, orange groves and flowers of semi-tropic valleys.

description_tesim: Artist Henry William Cannon, in his studio in the Lyceum Theater building, wearing smock, holding painting on table, with other paintings, some portraits, some floral, on table and on wall behind
This photograph appears with the article by Neeta Marquis, "True Spirit of Art Found in City." Los Angeles Times, 3 July 1927.

This biography from the Archives of AskART: Born in England on April 9, 1862. Cannon was educated in London and worked there as a designer for gold and silversmiths. He worked in NYC for several years before settling in Los Angeles about 1906. In 1912 he established the Cannon Art School in Los Angeles and taught there until 1933. He died in Redondo Beach, CA on Feb. 5, 1939. Member: Commercial Artists Ass'n of So. Calif.; Free Lance Art League of LA (co-founder and vice pres., 1918).
The Lyceum Theater was located at 227 S. Spring Street; it was demolished in 1941

title_tesim: Coastal view from hill towards house under construction in the Rancho Malibu la Costa development, Malibu, circa 1927
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View from coastal hill towards house under construction, the Pacific Coast Highway and beach far below in the area of the Rancho Malibu la Costa development. A dirt road is visible below the house.

Rancho Topanga Malibu Sequit was a 13,316-acre Spanish land grant given by Spanish Governor José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1804 to José Bartolomé Tapia. In 1892 Frederick Hastings Rindge and May Knight Rindge, purchased the rancho. In 1926, the Marblehead Land Company, formed by May Knight Rindge, made a $6 million deal with Harold G. Ferguson to develop the La Costa area (between Las Flores Canyon and Carbon Canyon), but that deal fell apart when Ferguson was sentenced to jail for securities fraud in 1931. Ferguson laid out the development with street names as they remain today: Rambla Vista, Paseo Serra, etc. It took until the mid 1930's for development of the area to begin in earnest.

title_tesim: Rosemary Chew and Olive Young standing in front of the Elk's Lodge No. 99, Los Angeles, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2357 P1-6. Pacific [illegible]. 1928. Pls ret negs. IN THE GARDEN OF THE WESTERN GODS. Olive Young, right, "Chinese Mary pickford" popular motion picture player of Chinese and oriental roles, Rosemary Chew, noted Chinese soprano and artist's model, photographed in the garden of the Otis Art Institute in Los Angeles with the new Elk's Club as a back ground. Showing frieze of heroes etc. on wall - thus, in a garden of the Western gods.
named_subject_tesim: Elks (Fraternal order) Lodge No. 99 (Los Angeles, Calif.)--Buildings
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
local_identifier_ssm: In the garden of the Western gods
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Olive Young was an actress who was a major star at the Great Wall studio in Shanghai from 1926 to 1929. Born Olive Young in St. Joseph, Missouri,  she first visited China at the age of 16, when her parents sent her to Hong Kong to complete her education. Two years later she began her acting career in Shanghai. After 1929, she returned to the United States and had a brief acting career in Hollywood.

Rosemary Chew and Olive Young standing hand in hand in front of the former Elk's Lodge No. 99 building (607 Park View Street) with arched windows, two angel statues and frieze near roof. The building is an outstanding example of Gothic Revival architecture style (Neo-Gothic), and was designed by Claud Beelman.
combined_subject_ssim: Elks (Fraternal order) Lodge No. 99 (Los Angeles, Calif.)--Buildings

title_tesim: Rock carving of Abraham Lincoln above cave entrance, Los Angeles, 1930
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2464.  P 1-2-3-4-5-6.  Cave.  Sawtelle.  Cave.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Mysterious cave, with plaque of Lincoln carved above entrance, and figures of Egyptians within, discovered recently by Boy Scouts in Santa Monica mountains near Sawtelle, California.  Officials at National Military Home there state the cave was work of colored veteran some 25 years ago.  The man would disappear for days in the hills.  He was known to have been a devotee of voodoo magic.  (Clarice Barclay stands outside door.)

description_tesim: The adobe of Manuel Dominguez, on the Mexican land grant of Rancho San Pedro, was completed in 1826. description_tesim: The adobe of Manuel Dominguez, on the Mexican land grant of Rancho San Pedro, was completed in 1826. title_tesim: Typewritten description of photos of Rancho Los Cerritos, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1850-1851-1852-1853-1854.  Front view of house.  [Struck through in pencil: Long Beach] 1929. 1929.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Juan Temple adobe house, 1838, at Los Cerritos, Long Beach, Calif.  Front view of house, also view of patio with Mike Mario, 116 yrs. old in 1930, conversing with fair visitors.  Mario has lived in the house going on 60 years.  (see story attached).  Bull fights held here 90 yrs. ago.

Text reads, in part: Still standing near the Virginia Country Club ... is the adobe house which Don Juan Temple built in 1844 as the spacious hacienda for his 27,000-acre ranch, which extended from the Pacific Ocean ... some 30 miles to the Sierra Madre mountains ... scenes of bull fights, rodeas [sic] and fiestas ... In 1866 Don Juan Temple sold his rancho ... to Mr. Llewellyn Bixby and Dr. Thomas Flint ... Mike Mario, age 115 ... has been a buckaroo in the employ of the ranch going on 60 years.

title_tesim: Typewritten document describing photographs of shipments of Easter lilies from Los Angeles, 1931
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Bartlett 1048 - 1049 - 1050 1051 - 1052 - 1053 Easter Lillies.  Easter Lillies, Los Angeles nursery, where 500,000, a million dollars worth of these blooms are coming into full flowering for the Easter trade.  Girls, left to right, loading flowers into truck - Rosine McDougall, Grace Wilkiemeyer, Marjorie Franklin.  Closeup, one girl, with flowers, Rosine McDougall.  Closeup, one girl, with Bob Lloyd (aviator), Grace Wilkiemeyer.

Text: 50,000 Easter lillies [sic – lilies] start moving from Sawtelle greenhouses of Armacost & Royston to flower stores of Pacific Coast and Mid-Western states. Expert hothouse workers have carefully regulated temperatures of the acres of glass-covered gardens in an effort to being [sic – bring] the plants to bloom promptly on Easter Sunday. This year due to the unusually warm spring sun a few hundred of the plants defied the efforts of workers and bloomed ahead of schedule. Some of these early lillies [sic – lilies]  went out over this week end as cut flowers etc. pictures -- Boy Loyd, commercial flyer, with package of Easter lilies destined Idaho, one of the season’s first shipments of blooms from Sawtelle gardens, ready to take off from near-by Clover field to relay package to plane of Western Airways Express at Vail Field. Miss Grace Wilkiemeyer, student at Harding High School, Sawtelle, delivered package to waiting plane. -----  Advance shipments of Easter lillies [sic – lilies] from Sawtelle hothouses going out by truck. Girls from Harding High School, Sawtelle, assisting, left to right – Rosine McKougall, Grace Wilkiemeyer, Marjorie Franklin.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.

title_tesim: Villa de Leon (Leon Kauffmann residence), terrace and coast view, Pacific Palisades, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 1459-1460-1461-1462, 8/8/29, Leon Kauffman home, Castellamarre
named_subject_tesim: Villa de Leon (Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Villa de Leon is located at 17948 Porto Marina Way. Designed by Kenneth MacDonald Jr. and built in 1927, it is one of the largest villas in Southern California.

Exterior view of the ocean front terrace and terrace facade of the Villa de Leon, and the stairs leading from the paved terrace to the garden terrace below. The terrace is paced in a checkerboard pattern of dark and light stone. There is a nude statue on a pedestal and the coast is visible beyond the terrace.
combined_subject_ssim: Villa de Leon (Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: Text describing a photograph of an Indian grinding mill, near Saddle Peak, Los Angeles, 1929
caption_tesim: Typewritten text reads: GRIST-MILL OF STONE AGE.  Among the oak trees in Las Flores Canyon in the Malibu region of Southern California, on the site of an ancient Indian settlement, is a great flat piece of sandstone, seven feet in diameter, and pitted by circular holes worn out by the stone pestles of the grinders.  Here the Indian women ground meal from acorns.

Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings

The National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers (NHDVS), Pacific Branch, was an earlier name, in part, for the United States Department of Veterans Affairs. Photographed on the grounds of what was later named the Greater Los Angeles Veterans Affairs Healthcare System (U.S.)
Long shot of mess hall, entrance and facade, with about 50 men gathered outside on dirt yard, standing, with palm tree in foreground
Building identification from www.nps.gov/nhl/themes/Special%20Studies/NHDVS/Pacific%20Branch.pdf

Date from National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers Assessment of Significance And National Historic Landmark Recommendations (www.nps.gov): The James W. Wadsworth Hospital opened in 1927 ... The next year, new barracks and a new mess hall were constructed.

description_tesim: Text reads: [Stamped] Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  [Handwritten] Hold this copy.  [Typewritten] Copies of photographs from the private family album of Mrs. Mary Van Ness Leavitt, 1337 15th Street, Santa Monica, California, sister of Herbert Hoover and Theodore J. Hoover.  These pictures are duplicated through the courtesy of Mrs. Leavitt who has supplied the information covering them on the enclosed caption sheet.1- Mary Minthorn, grandmother of the Hoover children on their mother’s side.2- Jesse Clark Hoover and Hulda Minthorn Hoover, parents of the Hoover children, photographed at West Branch, Iowa, between 1880 and 1889.3- Childhood home of the Hoover children at West Branch, Iowa, where the parents died.  Herbert Hoover’s mother (then age about 35) stands in the front yard of the house.  This house WAS NOT the birthplace of the Hoover children, according to Mrs. Leavitt.4- Standing, left, Theodore J. (“Tad”) Hoover, at age 20, and Herbert (“Bert”) C. Hoover, at 16; seated Mary (“MAY”) Hoover, age 14, now Mrs. Mary Van Ness Leavitt, Santa Monica, Calif.  This group of the Hoover children was made at Salem, Oregon, in 1888.  Theodore Hoover is now head of the engineering department at Stanford University; Herbert Hoover is [struck out] Secretary of Commerce [handwritten below] President of the United States.5- When Herbert Hoover was a junior at Stanford University -- left to right, Professor Newsome, then instructor in geology; J.K. Means, mining engineer; Herbert C. Hoover.6- Herbert Hoover and a classmate when both were juniors at Stanford University.7- Herbert Hoover as a freshman at Stanford University, age 17.8- Herbert Hoover at the age of 25, photographed at Perth, West Australia, in 1898, when he was serving as mining engineer for the Bewick Moreing Co. (verify name of mining company) of London.9- Herbert Hoover at the age of 34, after he had left Australia and was stationed at London.10--11--12--13  new studies of Mrs. Mary Van Ness Leavitt, only sister of Herbert Hoover, made at her home in Santa Monica, California

description_tesim: Herbert Hoover, about age 8, seated on stool, with his sister Mary Hoover (later Mary Van Ness Leavitt) seated on bench, brother Theodore Hoover standing behind them, copied from Hoover family album
Photograph of print, held with tack at right edge

A similar photograph, from about 1888-1890, appears with Los Angeles Times article, May 12, 1928, Glimpses at Secretary Hoover’s Youth Gleaned From Family Album. Out of an old family album in the possession of his sister, Mrs. Mary Van Ness Leavitt … were culled these old photographs giving an intimate glimpse into the early life of Herbert Hoover, Secretary of Commerce, and the outstanding candidate for the Republican nomination for the Presidency. … The Times is indebted to Mrs. Leavitt for the old photographs, reproduced below with a number of the most recent pictures taken of the Secretary.

description_tesim: The Hotel Windemere was located at 1431 Ocean Ave. Built in 1909, it was demolished in 1962. John Lindsay, a southern California architect, owned the building from at least 1954 until its demolition. According to newspaper reports from 1954 to 1956, he and Jane Wooster Scott were a couple.

Photograph of a painting in an American naïve style of pedestrians in 19th century dress strolling in front of "Barber Joe's" shop in a small town street. Signed "Wooster," the painting is an early work by Jane Wooster Scott (b. 1929). The painting hangs on a wall covered in striped wall paper at the Windemere Hotel.

Cast members from the Santa Monica Civic Opera production of "Hansel and Gretel" greet their audience. Two performers, male and female, wearing German-style clothing greet children in front of a production poster. The male, who portrayed Hansel and Gretel's father, is autographing playbills. Barbara Ludwig, as Gretel, stands to his right. She wears a dirndl, her hair styled in pigtails. A production poster is propped behind them. it reads, in part: "Hansel and Gretel. Irving  Beckman  conducting. Fri. Ja[nuary...], Sat. [January...]." Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Painting of a female nude by Mary Angela Davis, 1950-1965
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Angla Davis artist
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Impressionist style painting of a female nude  by artist Mary Angela Davis.

In image ark no. 21198/zz002d454b, the artist holds a painting with a partially visible signature "Mary Angel..." and the negative sleeve bears the name "Angla Davis." The Los Angeles Times has 2 notices of an artist named Mary Angela Davis exhibiting: in a show called "Five Artists" at the Galleries Perreau Saussine, 7768 Santa Monica Blvd ("Art Events This Week," Los Angeles Times, 14 Feb. 1960: G8) and the Thora Gallery at 5504 S. Sepulveda in Culver City (Los Angeles Times 10 Mar. 1963: W2). Title  Mary Angela Davis photographed with three of her paintings, 1950-1965     AltTitle      Alt ID.local :  uclamss_1300_2553          Subject  Artists $x American $z California $z Los Angeles          Name.repository :  University of California, Los Angeles. $b Library Special Collections     Name.photographer :  Bartlett, Adelbert, 1887-1966     Name.artist :  Davis, Mary Angela, $d b. 1928     Name.subject :  Davis, Mary Angela, $d b. 1928          Description.fundingNote :  Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds. Description.note :  There are notices for 2 of Mary Angela Davis exhibitions in the Los Angeles times: "Five Artists" at the Galleries Perreau Saussine, 7768 Santa Monica Blvd ("Art Events This Week," Los Angeles Times, 14 Feb. 1960: G8) and the Thora Gallery at 5504 S. Sepulveda in Culver City (Los Angeles Times 10 Mar. 1963: W2). Davis applied for Naturalization in Los Angeles on June 13, 1958 and listed as her address 11216 Barman Ave., Culver City.

title_tesim: Dedication of Camp Josepho, Pacific Palisades, 1941
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2910, Camp Josepho, Boy Scouts, 6/7/41, Leo Carrillo, Dedication.
named_subject_tesim: Camp Josepho (Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Group photograph with Leo Carrillo (fifth from left) and Anatol Josepho (possibly), and a host of men, women and children during the dedication of Camp Josepho, a Boy Scouting Camp in the Pacific Palisades. The Crescent Bay Council flag is held up with an American flag by two scouts partially hidden amongst the crowd whose members include military men in uniform, Scoutmasters, and men wearing Plains Indian headdresses. Boy scouts line the bottom row. Also, the woman wearing a wide brimmed hat (at right) is likely Leo Carrillo’s wife, Edith Shakespeare Carrillo.
Related to the Los Angeles Times article, "Scouts Receive Camp as Gift: Donors Watch Boys of Crescent Bay Council Enjoy New Facilities," 8 Jun. 1941:18.
combined_subject_ssim: Camp Josepho (Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles, Calif.)

This image is of cast member Pearl Jennings who played the role of Belle, the maid, in the production of the Sheldon Sheppard play "O.K. By Me." Jennings is wearing a dark dress, necklace, earrings, and bracelet. [.

Louise Glaum was the director of this production of "O.K. By Me," a play by Sheldon Shepard. Mrs. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director, who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first "vamp" actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.

title_tesim: Gordone De Lagniel at the Ebell Club, Los Angeles, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Miss Gordone D'Lagniel [sicl], Donoho, 3100 west Adams St, Child Estate 6/26/42, Shakespere [sic]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This photograph is probably related to the article "Ebell Juniors Slate Carnival and Dance; Bishop Stevens Will Discuss Shakespeare." 18 Apr. 1942: A6.

Gordone De Lagniel (born Mayes Dickens Donoho and also known as Maggie), was a singer and socialite, active in the 1940s and 1950s. She is mentioned performing singing in various L.A. Times articles during this period. A 1941 city directory for Los Angeles lists her as Gordone DeLagniel, and gives her profession as Musician, and her address as 3100 West Adams Blvd.

Gordone De Lagniel at the Ebell dressed  perform Shakespearean songs for the annual luncheon to celebrate the birthday of Shakespeare. She is wearing a Shakespearean-inspired costume with an ostrich feather-trimmed hat, a fur-trimmed shawl, gown and parasol. Behind her is flight of stairs. The Ebell is located at 743 S. Lucerne Blvd.

description_tesim: This image is a copy of a page of an article, "A Californian in the Holy Land", written by Adelbert Bartlett in 1929. This article covers his trip through Beirut, Jerusalem, and Palestine. He traveled with a professor, who is not named. Various locals guided them on their trip, as documented in article. Once arriving in Beirut via ship, they traveled primarily by car.
This page contains information on Bartlett's time in Nazareth. This page has an image of children wearing traditional Arabic and Lebanese garb.  There is also an image of the Dome of the Rock.

title_tesim: View of the back of Sorrento Club from Palisades Park cliffs, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Mr. Philbin, Sorrento Beach Club 1952, S.M. Pool.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.

This image is a view of the back of the Sorrento Beach Club taken from the Palisade Park cliffs above. Three men can be seen on the roof, two holding up wooden beams, and the third hammering something on a pier of the building. The beach and ocean can be seen in the background.

Miss Shirley R. Doman was the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Roberta Shaw Doman of Pacific Palisades. She attended University High School and then UCLA and was an Alpha Chi Omega sorority sister. She married John Axel Michelson in 1946.

Text from negative sleeve: Robert Major school, 1949

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: The Robert Major School of acting was located in Beverly Hills, as noted in directories from 1929 and 1930.

Several members of the Robert Majors School of Acting performing in a play. Pairs of men and women express varied emotions and actions while a young man and older woman sit at a table and partake in a meal. A younger woman serves them from a metal coffee pot while he smears butter on a slice of bread. The setting is that of a kitchen in dwelling. Torn wall paper exposes the brick wall beneath. A stove and sink are visible on the right. Also visible are curtained windows and a door (a coat hangs next to it on the right). Additional set props include a light-colored hutch (on the far right), a linen-covered table and chairs. Beyond the stage, several audience members are viewed at foreground.

title_tesim: Dean Cornwell, painter, standing before the Los Angeles Central Library, Los Angeles, 1932
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2334 p 1 to 14, 1932, Los Angeles Library, Dean Cornwell, Artist, 206 E. 33rd St NY city, Los Angeles Library.
named_subject_tesim: Los Angeles Public Library. Central Library
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

ark:/21198/zz002d4mdr (document)
ark:/21198/zz002d4mf8  (document)
description_tesim: Portrait of painter Dean Cornwell standing beside the reflecting pool in of the front of the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library. At the time he was working on his mural, the "Four Great Eras of California History," located on the walls beneath the rotunda. Cornwell is wearing a suit and tie, holding a briefcase in one hand. In his other hand is a lit cigarette. One of his feet is raised, resting on the edge of a fountain. Behind him, the entrance to the library can be seen.

Dean Cornwell was an American painter and illustrator who was prominent in the early 20th century. His works include illustrations for Harper's Bazaar, Cosmopolitan, and Good Housekeeping. He also painted several other murals besides those for the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library, including for the locations: the Warwick Hotel New York, 10 Rockefeller Plaza New York, and the Sevier State Office Building in Tennessee. He is mentioned in several L.A. Times articles, including one dated June 21, 1931, which covers his research process for the murals for the L.A. library.
combined_subject_ssim: Los Angeles Public Library. Central Library

View of painter Dean Cornwell seated at a table with a sketch and holding a paint brush and a pallet (or more sketches) in the rotunda room at the Los Angeles Public (Central) Library. Cornwell is seen in the process of planning his mural titled "Four Great Eras of California History" which was to be painted on the walls below the rotunda. Cornwell is looking up at the rotunda which is surfaced with an elaborate mosaic.

title_tesim: Santa Monica Beach and Yacht Harbor from Palisades Park, Santa Monica, circa 1946
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2824 P31 to 35. Santa Monica. Views from Jones Monument to yacht harbor. See also file #2600.  Show complete file #.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2824 P 33
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

Photograph of a man and woman standing at a rustic eucalyptus branch fence overlooking the Santa Monica Bay, with an additional woman walking toward the camera at foreground. The path follows a stretch of beach populated with people relaxing on the sand and frolicking in the ocean. Camera view towards the Yacht Harbor and Pier with boats viewed at right. The image is framed by a large palm tree at foreground. Photographed near John P. Jones Monument, Palisades Park.

John P. Jones Monument, located within Palisades Park at Ocean Avenue and Wilshire Boulevard, Santa Monica. The monument is dedicated to Nevada Senator John P. Jones, co-founder of the town of Santa Monica with Colonel Robert S. Baker, 1875. Coordinates derived from this location.

This photo (labeled 2824 P 33) may have been taken around the same time as nine photograph negatives of Palisades Park and Santa Monica Beach labeled “2824 P 20 to 30.” Text from associated negative sleeve: 2824 P 20 to 30. Santa Monica, From Palisades Park. 1946. Wide angle. Show full file #.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

subject_tesim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)
description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

Photograph of a woman seated near the edge of a Palisades Park cliff. Two large palm trees stand nearby on the right.  The cliffs continue across from the woman at left, with a wooden rail fence and trees in view. View faces south towards a stretch of Pacific Coast Highway (California Highway 1), with cars lining the road and parked in lots near what are presumably beach homes, clubs and businesses. These structures follow along the Highway and beach- the beach populated by crowds on the sand and in the water. At center sits the Santa Monica Pier and Yacht Harbor, marked by several buildings and numerous nearby ships. A large building and several other structures line its pier.  Beyond the pier, the coastline continues into the distance. Photographed at the foot of Montana Avenue, above the Sorrento Beach Club.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica State Beach Park (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Inscription of General Don Diego de Vargas, El Morro National Monument, New Mexico, circa 1898
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Old history. Arizona – California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Copy print photograph of an inscription made by General Don Diego de Vargas, carved into Inscription Rock, El Morro National Monument. The inscription, written in Spanish, is one of numerous imprints captured in the photograph. Translation: “Here was the General Don Diego de Vargas, who conquered for our Holy Faith and for the Royal Crown all the New Mexico, at his own expense, year of 1692.” A second complete caption sits at left, reading: “AMSON, July 8, 1858, Ohio.” Inscriptions photographed by George Wharton James.
Caption notes that the image was copyrighted by George Wharton James in 1898. Copy print date unknown.

Copy print photograph of a round woven granary on a raised platform made of logs, with cloth satchels resting beneath the platform.  The granary is surrounded by soil and shrubbery – landscape features that continue into the background.

Related to Los Angeles Times article, June 25, 1950, Old-Time Cars to Revive Noted Santa Monica Race.  Santa Monica’s famous road race for the Vanderbilt Cup will be revived Sunday, July 16, when 12 old-time race cars roar around a two-mile course … It will be the first time the event has been revived since the seven years from 1910 to 1916. …

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" cast members Kay Marshall, Natalie Garrotto, June Moss and 2 others, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 146
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. This is possibly the December 3rd performance with Kay Marshall in the role of Gilda. The role alternated between Natalie Garrotto, Kay Marshall and Diana Guadagno. Enrico Porta played Rigoletto for all four shows.

Photograph of female cast members in costume. An unidentified woman hands a bouquet of flowers to Kay Marshall, who portrayed Gilda in the production on December 3, 1949. To Marshall’s right are Natalie Garrotto (who played Gilda the following night), an unidentified singer (as Countess Ceprano), and June Moss. Photographed on stage, behind the curtain, with a portion of the set in background.

Don Shaw Trio publicity photograph. title_tesim: "Rigoletto" production cast members "Sparafucile" and "Maddalena" (Gladys Andrews), John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 152
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of two cast members with the Santa Monica Civic Opera, in costume as Sparafucile (a hired assassin) and his sister, Maddalena (portrayed by Gladys Andrews). Both wear Romani (Gypsy) costumes. He wears a striped shirt and vest, a scarf covering his head, and an earring in one ear. Scars are drawn on his face and arms. She wears an off-the-shoulder peasant blouse with wide belt, necklaces and bracelets of beads and coins, and a flower behind one ear.

description_tesim: Photograph of three cast members, one male and two females, with the Santa Monica Civic Opera, in costume. From left: Louis Statham, performing as Sparafucile, wears a Gypsy or pirate-style outfit with a loose shirt, vest, waist sash, pirate pants and boots. The outfit is completed with a scarf tied around his head and an earring in one ear. The two females wear satin gowns with fitted bodices, lace collars, and puffed sleeves. The photograph was taken in a hallway at John Adams Auditorium.

description_tesim: Van Nuys Opera Association related performances are noted in the Los Angeles Times, 1957-1965.

Photograph of three cast members from a Van Nuys Opera Association production, probably for the opera "Carmen." The performers are dressed in traditional Spanish attire. They wear mantillas with peinetas (high combs worn at the crown of the head), and hold hand fans. The women stand in front a wall with faux brick design and a poster featuring the image of a toreador and bull in an arena. Text at top of poster reads: "Plaza de Toros de Salamanca."

title_tesim: Students work on school paper SaMoJaC, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2948 Santa Monica Jr. College Samojac Dummy E.R.Coulson 1937.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image is of a group of Santa Monica College students working on the school paper, the SaMoJaC. Some students sit at desks with typewriters before them. Another student points to the large image of a mock front page plastered on the building behind them. Still others stand with books and papers in their hands. The SaMoJac was first printed in 1929, and would change its name to the Corsair in 1945. E.R Coulson was a professor at Santa Monica College who oversaw the printing of the paper in the late 1930s.

description_tesim: Adelbert Bartlett photographed members of the Santa Monica Music Guild, images dated 1949-1953.

Photograph of two women and one man, captured seated and standing in a residential living room. They are likely members of an unidentified opera guild (possibly the Santa Monica Music Guild). The male stands over one woman who holds a travel brochure with texts reading, "World Adventure Series" and "The Grand Tour" [remaining titles out of focus]. At center stands a three-paneled dividing screen is posted with advertisements, photographs, and clippings related to concert performances by Mantovani (Annunzio Paolo Mantovani, 1905–1980), the Vienna Boys Choir (or Vienna Choir Boys), "Gay 90s Night" at the Hollywood Bowl, and Roberto Iglesias (Spanish dancer).

title_tesim: Los Angeles County Sheriff Eugene Biscailuz speaking at Cabrillo Day ceremony at Palisades Park, Santa Monica, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Santa Monica. 2932. Cabrillo Day. Oct 1942 10/25/42. Pioneer Society unveiling plaque in Palisades Park at foot of California Ave. Eugen [sic] Biscailuz. Sheriff in view.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d5ksd (document)
description_tesim: For photo order document, see "Relation" note.

Photograph of Los Angeles County Sheriff Eugene Biscailuz speaking at an event hosted by the Pioneer Society at Palisades Park. He and the crowd in attendance are there to celebrate the unveiling of a monument commemorating the 400th anniversary of the Juan Cabrillo’s landing at Santa Monica Bay, 8 October 1542. Biscailuz stands at a microphone, on a platform, with several seated men (seven in view). The stone marker stands covered behind the platform, adjacent to a railroad crossing sign. Male and female attendees sit on benches at foreground, while a band of musicians wearing uniforms can be seen seated left of the dais. At right, parked cars and palm trees line the street. More palm trees, a portion of the park’s branch fence, and the California coastline comprise the background view. Photographed at Palisades Park, Santa Monica.

The other officials, probably members of the Pioneer Society, are unidentified. Photographed at Palisades Park, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Julia Stuart is noted as a dance instructor in several Santa Monica directories, dates ranging 1947-1960. Addresses include 2721 Wilshire Blvd. (1954 directory), and 2315 Wilshire Blvd. (1960 directory).

Seven young ballet students of Julia Stuart photographed in a park. Six girls wearing Grecian-style costumes are seated on the lawn as one stands in a balletic pose behind the group. She wears a leotard with sash at waist, tights and ballet flats. The photograph may have been taken at Douglas Park (2439 Wilshire Blvd., Santa Monica).
Two additional photographs feature these seven students wearing the same outfits, performing/posing in a dance studio (image ark. nos. 21198/zz002d5wzb, and 21198/zz002d5x5x).

title_tesim: “Marriage of Figaro” production with June Moss, Caterina Micieli, Roderick Risto, and Pryor Bowen, Barnum Hall Theatre, Santa Monica, 1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 3/22/58. Figaro.
named_subject_tesim: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, 1756-1791. Nozze di Figaro
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ballet dancers and opera singers in a Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of Act 3 of “The Marriage of Figaro.” The Count and Countess Almaviva (portrayed by Pryor Bowen and June Moss) sit upstage on two thrones set on a dais. They watch along with Figaro and Susanna (Roderick Risto and Caterina Micieli), who stand at the Countess’ right side, and others as two peasants dance at center. The aristocrats wear 18th century style attire – coats with deep cuffs, waistcoats (vests), breeches and stockings. The Count and Countess both wear wigs – she with a pompadour hairstyle, he in a powdered wig. Figaro’s bride Susanna wears attire similar to the peasant women who wear full skirts with overskirts tied at the waist, peasant blouses covered with lace-up bodices, and caps covering their hair, but Susanna wears a veil. The peasant men wear coats, breeches, stockings, and cravats. The setting is a hall in Count Almaviva’s palace. Musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, 1756-1791. Nozze di Figaro

title_tesim: “Marriage of Figaro” production with Lou Ann Wyckoff, Roderick Risto, Pryor Bowen, and Robert Mahoney, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 3/22/58. Figaro.
named_subject_tesim: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, 1756-1791. Nozze di Figaro
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
