dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of  Act 1 of “The Marriage of Figaro.” Figaro (portrayed by Roderick Risto) stands center and sings while Count Almaviva (singer Pryor Bowen), his page Cherubino (played by female singer Lou Ann Wyckoff), Don Basilio (Robert Mahoney) and several cast members portraying peasants listen. The aristocrats wear 18th century style attire – coats with deep cuffs, waistcoats (vests), breeches and stockings. Figaro wears a shirt with lace collar and cuffs. The peasant women wear full skirts with overskirts tied at the waist, peasant blouses covered with lace-up bodices, and caps covering their hair. The men wear coats, breeches and cravats. The setting is a sparsely furnished room in Count Almaviva’s palace, with ornately painted walls, an end table, chair and footstool. Balcony shot allows for views of the entire stage, musicians in and outside of the orchestra pit, and numerous audience members. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
combined_subject_ssim: Mozart, Wolfgang Amadeus, 1756-1791. Nozze di Figaro

title_tesim: Nelda Scarsella and others performing “La Traviata” at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Frank Pace. Opera Traviata, 9/15/51.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 15
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 1. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta, wearing a tiered gown with lace trim and holding a feathered accoutrement. She walks away from a group of three men, including singer Gene Curtsinger (who portrays Alfredo Germont) at far right.  She makes her way across the stage to an unidentified performer portraying Barone Dauphol (wearing a monocle and sash across his chest) and a second male. Additional cast members, wearing gowns and coattails, converse amongst themselves in the background. The setting is a salon in Violetta’s Parisian home. The set’s design and props reflect an opulent space, including curtained windows, upholstered chairs, and a chandelier hanging above a table set with a lace table cloth, candelabra, plates, and champagne goblets. Beyond the stage, a portion of the string section can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.

Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

title_tesim: Otis Garth from “O.K. By Me,” directed by Louise Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 8
Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum Play Hollywood, 1952. Also Peggy O’ Connell, Otis Garth.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Actor Otis Garth, who portrayed the lead protagonist, Nelson Withers, photographed on the set of “O.K. By Me.”

Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.
Male cast members included Otis Garth (portraying Nelson Withers), Wally Norman (Gregory Wilson) and Stanley Herbert Briggs (Redmond Meadows).
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

description_tesim: Verdi’s “La Traviata,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, September 15, 1951. Several notices were published in the Los Angeles Times. A critique appeared in the article, “’La Traviata’ Staged on Verdi Anniversary,” 17 Sep. 1951: B8

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 2, Scene 1. Opera singer Nelda Scarsella portrays Violetta (wearing a gown with ruffled trim and floral pick-ups). She sings seated with Enrico Porta (in the role of Giorgio Germont) on a garden bench. The setting is an exterior space at Violetta’s country home outside of Paris. A large tree, shrubs, and garden furniture complete the outdoor scenery. Beyond the stage at foreground, a musician playing a double-bass can be viewed in the orchestra pit.

Dancers dressed as Gypsies perform for party guests in a Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “La Traviata,” Act 2, Scene 2.  Cast members, wearing gowns and coattails, watch as the dancers perform center stage. The setting is a room in a Parisian home. The set’s design and props reflect an opulent space, including decorative wall treatments, a curtained window, upholstered chairs and settee, and a chandelier hanging above the center space. View from orchestra pit.
[Double exposed negative.]

title_tesim: Cosmopolitan Hotel, San Diego, between 1930-1945
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2805. San Diego. Old Town historical sites. Bowers museum. Juan Bandini. Old Town.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View of the Cosmopolitan Hotel (Casa de Bandini) in Old Town San Diego State Historic Park when it was owned by Cave Couts, Jr.

The Cosmopolitan Hotel is located at the southeast corner of the plaza in Old Town San Diego Historic Park. Originally the Casa de Bandini, it was constructed in 1827-1829 as a single-story adobe residence. It became a hotel in 1869 when a wood frame 2nd floor was added, and was an olive factory from 1900-1919. This photograph was taken sometime after the 1930 remodel in the steamboat revival style popular in Louisiana with vertical lath screens along the top of the porch and balcony. James and Nora Cardwell, who purchased the building in 1945, remodeled it in the Spanish Colonial style. Its restoration to its 1872 state, as the Cosmopolitan Hotel, was completed around 2010.

title_tesim: Raucous World War I veterans sing for the camera, Los Angeles, 1919
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 3003. World War Vets of Siberia, home from front, in Los Angeles, year 1919, on way home to Camp Dodge, Iowa sing song specially improvised for Red Cross wkrs who fed them Yank cof. and donuts – ‘I want’s go home/ the Scechs are disgusted, / The Rooshuns are sore/ I Don’t want’ noninterfere any more / “Take me over the sea / Where the Bolsheviki / Cannot Get me / Oh my, I don’t want’s die / I want’s go home.”
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: These veterans were probably part of the American Expeditionary Force Siberia, a United States Army force involved in the Russian Civil War during the end of World War I, from 1918 to 1920.

Copy print photograph of World War I veterans singing a song improvised during their time in Siberia. The soldiers wear fatigues with either a campaign hat or an overseas hat (a cap with a narrow tapered crown), and boots with canvas leggings. Two wear button-down tunics with high collars. They are gathered in front of an unidentified building.

caption_tesim: Text  from negative sleeve: 3007. Los Angeles Fiesta, crowning queen early 1900's??
Handwritten on edge of negative: Reversed

description_tesim: Copy print photograph of the Fiesta de Los Angeles Queen at her coronation. The queen wears a paneled jacquard print dress with long gloves, necklace and tiara. Female ushers, wearing men's 17th century courtly attire, carry the train of her velvet cape. At left stands a man wearing a three-piece suit with a tall, stiff collar and narrow necktie. With exception of the females who follow her, only males are visible in the crowd. All are dressed like the man who greets the queen except for one police officer wearing a roll-collar coat with a button-down front, fabric trimmed cuffs, badge and hat with wreath-style emblem at center. Three buildings can be viewed in the background, one decorated with bunting draped over its window sills.

title_tesim: Sunset Apartments, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: New up and down apt house. Sunset Blvd, near Bernheimer Gardens, 1952.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The apartment complex was covered in the article, "On a Pacific Hillside: Leapfrog Apartments." House and Home, April 1, 1952, 101-03.

Photograph of the Sunset Apartments (alternately named Leapfrog Apartments), side and rear composite view. The modern apartment house sits on a hillside overlooking the ocean; the building steps down the hillside following the angle of its slope. Multiple palms, shrubs and other plantings surround the complex. Large stones have been utilized as landscape features. The remaining landscape in view is barren by comparison. Signs on the property read: "Vagtborg Construction Co. General Contractors, ...714 San Vicente Blvd. AR.. 7-4274," "Kenneth N. Lind A.I.A. architect, 943 N La Cienega Blvd., Crewtview 5-1010," and "J. A. Gooch Landscape Architect, ...San Vicente AR 9-02..."

The apartment complex, built in 1952, was designed by Kenneth Nels Lind; J.A. (Jesse Alexander) Gooch was the landscape architect. The complex’s floor-to-ceiling windows and stepped design insured that each apartment had its own unencumbered views of the Pacific Ocean and Pacific Palisades hills.

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: View of the commencement ceremony in which Caroline Bartlett Farnham (daughter of Adelbert Bartlett) participated. Photographed at the campus Open Air Theater, viewed from a hill above the students and ceremony attendees.

UCLA commencement ceremonies were held at the campus Open Air Theater from 1941 to 1949. The Open Air Theater (sometimes referred to as the Greek Theater) was located at the south end of campus where the Public Health Building stands today. The Open Air Theater was bulldozed for the expanding campus in 1952.

title_tesim: Ana Bégué de Packman with demolition workers, Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda, Los Angeles, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2554. 12/20/40. Mrs. Beague [sic] Packman. Old adobe house. Bundy Drive WLA, 1940. See also Bartlett earlier photos …enclosed.
named_subject_tesim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda was an adobe home an adobe home located near San Vicente Springs at South Bundy Drive and Nebraska Ave. José Dolores Sepúlveda, b. 1826-1905, was the son of Francisco Sepúlveda, holder of the original land grant for Rancho San Vicente y Santa Monica. The home was once part of Rancho San Vicente.

Ana Bégué de Packman served as Secretary of the Historical Society of Southern California, and was a charter member of the First Families of California. She was a descendant of Juan Francisco Reyes, alcalde (municipal magistrate) for the Pueblo de Los Angeles (1790 and 1793-95). She was also a descendant of Maximo Alaniz, who owned much of what is now Westwood.

A young man loads adobe bricks onto a flatbed truck, with Ana Bégué de Packman and additional demolition workers in background. Packman, in overcoat and hat, stands atop a pile of rubble next to the adobe structure, holding a pencil and folded envelope. Her purse rests on the remnants of a wall. Wooden slats and other refuse are propped against partially spackled walls. Two workers are perched above the structure; one working, the other looking in Packman’s direction.  A neighborhood dotted with utility poles and numerous trees are viewed in the background.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: Vengeance dive bomber, Downey, circa 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Historic file. Los Angeles, World War II, "old" plane by 1940's. Corsair?
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Vultee Aviation Manufacturing Company, and its airfield (Vultee Field), was located in Downey, California. The Vultee facility manufactured Valiant basic trainers, the Vanguard P-66 pursuit plane, and the Vengeance A-31 dive bomber.  Britain first ordered 200 Vengeance bombers in July 1940, with an additional order later that year. Vultee continued producing the bomber until 1944.

Copy print of a published image with caption that features two test pilots wearing flight suits, helmets, and parachutes strapped to their backs. They speak to a third man in uniform while a bomber plane is being loaded behind them. Caption reads: "Bomber for Britain - Like a wartime scene on an English airdrome is this evening picture of a new Vengeance dive bomber being loaded with practice bombs at Vultee Field as the waiting pilots stand by. This Vultee dive bomber, now in production for Britain, is designed to outperform any known plane of similar type in world."
Date of published photograph or of this reproduction is unknown.

title_tesim: Comedic dancer posed with props, Santa Monica, 1948
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 11/1/48. Comedy Dancer c/o Julia Stuart. Man.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The unidentified dancer may have been a student or colleague of Julia Stuart, dance instructor.

Photograph of a male comedic dancer (wearing a striped shirt, jacket, pants and a coil of yarn for a hat) stands behind a desk with one foot propped atop boxes on a step stool. He stirs the contents of an empty glass with one hand while pointing to two stuffed monkeys with the other. The absurdist set is complete with additional toys: a plastic cat replaces a light bulb (seen at left with the lamp shade and a toy horse); a stuffed bear stands a foot away from a portrait of a young blond. A stack of books rests at center. The photograph was taken at Adelbert Bartlett's home (535 15th Street).

description_tesim: Fort Ross is a former Russian establishment in Sonoma County, California. title_tesim: Ana Bégué de Packman with demolition workers, Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda, Los Angeles, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2554. 12/20/40. Mrs. Beague [sic] Packman. Old adobe house. Bundy Drive WLA, 1940. See also Bartlett earlier photos …enclosed.
named_subject_tesim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ana Bégué de Packman stands atop a pile of rubble next to what remains of the Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda. She, in overcoat and hat, rests against the structure, while a worker sits atop a wall of adobe bricks. A second man stands against the wall at right. Remnants of spackle remain attached to the wall, while wooden rafters and door frames remain intact.

The Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda was an adobe home an adobe home located near San Vicente Springs at South Bundy Drive and Nebraska Ave. José Dolores Sepúlveda, 1826-1905, was the son of Francisco Sepúlveda, holder of the original land grant for Rancho San Vicente y Santa Monica. The home was once part of Rancho San Vicente.

Ana Bégué de Packman served as Secretary of the Historical Society of Southern California, and was a charter member of the First Families of California. She was a descendant of Juan Francisco Reyes, alcalde (municipal magistrate) for the Pueblo de Los Angeles (1790 and 1793-95). She was also a descendant of Maximo Alaniz, who owned much of what is now Westwood.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)

description_tesim: The Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda was an adobe home an adobe home located near San Vicente Springs at South Bundy Drive and Nebraska Ave. José Dolores Sepúlveda, b. 1826-1905, was the son of Francisco Sepúlveda, holder of the original land grant for Rancho San Vicente y Santa Monica. The home was once part of Rancho San Vicente.

View of the Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda in disrepair. Portions of spackle remain on exposed adobe brick walls. The home’s roof is covered with wooden shingles. Wooden slats rest against the walls, while a wooden door (or side of a crate) rest against a window.  A utility pole stands next to a wooden trough at foreground, amidst shrubs and weeds. At right, a ladder, clothes line with pins, and a metal washbasin can be viewed. At left, two young boys sit on the back of a flatbed truck, possibly children of the residents.

The home was once part of Rancho San Vicente.

A young woman stands with a toddler at the Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda. The unidentified woman (wearing a skirt, blouse, sweater and wedge-heeled shoes) stands within a wood-framed doorway of the dilapidated structure. Burlap sacks and wooden slats (or fencing) rest against the exposed adobe brick walls. One section of the wall is gutted. Above, the roof’s rafters are exposed, with portions of the roof missing all-together. Power lines cross overhead.
combined_subject_ssim: Casa de Don Dolores Sepúlveda (Los Angeles, Calif.)

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” rehearsal performance, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
Handwritten on edge of negative: 26
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers in a dress rehearsal of “The Merry Widow,” Act 1. A female singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari) performs at center stage while a member of the stage crew makes adjustments behind her. The remaining cast, wearing evening gowns and tuxedos, line up across the stage and watches her perform. An additional cast member dressed as an attendant stands to the far right. He wears a powdered wig, full sleeve shirt and waistcoat. The scene is a ball being held at the fictional Marsovian (Pontevedrian) embassy in Paris. Decorative set pieces with intricate floral designs flank a faux marble staircase. Above the stage, microphones can be seen hanging from behind a border curtain. Beyond the stage, musicians and the conductor can be seen in the orchestra pit at foreground.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” production with Natalie Garrotto, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 22
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.

description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. Singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hannah Glawari) stands center stage. . She and the other female performers (including Natalie Garrotto portraying Natalie (Valencienne), seated at the table on the left) wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country, including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. Cast members stand and behind her as she sings. The setting is a garden at Hannah’s home. Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table, chairs and settee.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

description_tesim: Keys Ranch is located within Joshua Tree National Park, San Bernardino County, Twentynine Palms vicinity. The arrastra (Spanish spelling is arrastre) and stamp mill described in the article remains on view to visitors.

Date range derived from the working dates of Philip Fein, whose name is noted above two photos on the left-hand page. Fein was an architectural photographer who worked primarily during the 1950s and 60s.

Photographed page from an unidentified publication with illustrations and text providing examples of mining equipment and petroglyphs found in the Mojave Desert area and at Keys Ranch. Text describing  illustration at top: "Gold mining and cattle running were the earliest activities of white men in the national monument area, as witness this early, crude stamp mill which contributes to the Zane Grey Western setting at Keys Ranch, formerly a working cattle ranch." [Text below center illustration:] "Even before Americans discovered this area, Mexican miners were crushing ore with crude arrastres built of native stone and motivated by mule or burro or manpower. This is a typical example found in Mojave Desert area, also at Keys Ranch."[Text for final illustration:] Sketches of petroglyphs - ancient rock incised writings -as seen at Coyote Hole, 200 yards from end of road and 50 feet up on wall of canyon. These Indian doodles, usually found at camps, waterholes, and passes, are widespread in Mojave Desert."

title_tesim: “Merry Widow” rehearsal performance, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 21
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 3. Seven singers (including Kay Marshall at far left) wearing satin gowns and feathered headdresses, perform while fellow cast members watch from tables situated on stage. Remaining cast members include women dressed as grisettes (can-can dancers), and men in tuxedos. Beyond the stage, casually dressed musicians can be seen in the orchestra pit at foreground. The minimal set and casual dress of at least one performer (seen wearing a sweater over her gown), as well as the number of audience members on view (two), suggests that this is a rehearsal performance.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.

description_tesim: The birth date in the Library of Congress authorized heading for Warner Weidler does not match the Social Security death index (Warner Alfred Weidler; b. 16 Jun 1922; d. 1 Dec 2010) or the 1940 U.S. Census, "abt 1923."

Photograph of George, Warner, and Walter Weidler of the Weidler Brothers Orchestra, all wearing dress slacks with long blazers,  ties and boutonnieres. The dark molding at the base of the wall of two other photographs taken on the same occasion indicates that the photograph was most probably taken at the home of Adelbert Bartlett (535 15th Street).

The Weidler Brothers Orchestra was a Los Angeles-based band featuring three saxophone playing siblings: George (1926-1989), Warner (1922-1910) and Walter (1923-1910). Their youngest sister, Virginia Weidler (1927-1968), was a child actress popular during the 1930s and 1940s.

title_tesim: “Pagliacci” production, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Pagliacci, 1952. SM Opera. [Image ark 21198/zz002d6xkk]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Pagliacci.” The stage is filled with cast members in peasant costumes, as performers portraying Canio/ Pagliacci (in clown costume) and Canio’s wife Nedda ride onstage on a horse drawn wagon. The setting is a 19th century Calabrian village. With rolling hills and trees painted on a backdrop. To the fore stands a secondary stage resembling a Greek temple. Drawn curtains shield the interior from view. A banner painted with “I Pagliacci” is attached to the pediment.  Beyond the stage, musicians can be viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

There is a column with gilded leaves in the right foreground.

title_tesim: Typescript document describing James B. Lockwood, whom Bartlett photographed, 1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2946. San Francisco. Nob Hill, Bay Bridge, Palo Alto (Stanford), Carmel.

Text reads, in part: [Pencil:] Photographed 12/4/34. [Typescript:] Adelbert Bartlett, 1572 West Pico Blvd, Los Angeles, Cal... A Chicago Cubs Player of the "Gay Nineties." Santa Monica, Calif. - - James B. Lockwood, thirty-five years a resident of this city, served as pitcher and second baseman for the original Chicago Cubs baseball team some forty years ago under the tutelage of the late Capt. Anson, "father of baseball." Lockwood, [handwritten] who says he is "65 years young" [typescript] to this day takes great delight in following the fortunes of the "great American game." Frequently he attends local semiprofessional games, and is preparing now to make his annual pilgrimage to Catalina Island, 25 miles off Los Angeles Harbor, to watch the present-day Chicago Cubs work in spring practice. The late William Wrigley, owner of the Cubs, when he was at home on Catalina Island would [handwritten] often [typescript]personally see that Lockwood was shown to a good sea at the training field... Lockwood's [handwritten] eldest [typescript] son, Leonard Louis, a product of local high school and college diamonds, encouraged by his father's example, has developed [handwritten] himself [typescript] into a pitcher of such ability that he has been signed by the Los Angeles "Angels" of the Pacific Coast league to report for active duty in March of 1935...
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002d7c11 (print)
ark:/21198/zz002d7c0h (print)
ark:/21198/zz002d7c2j (print)
description_tesim: Descriptions in this document match a different negative sleeve and its associated photographs. Text reads: From Adelbert Bartlett, 1572 West Pico, Los Angeles. 2 negs. 2635 P1 to 3. James B. Lockwood, Santa Monica, Calif. Served as pitcher and second baseman with orig. Chicago Cubs forty years ago under Capt. Anson. Now a gardener and as enthusiastic about it as he is of baseball. His son will shortly become a professional baseball player with Angels of Pacific Coast League. Lockwood is shown in pictures with a group of his young friends with whom he frequently plays on near-by sand lot when he is not attending to his garden. (for related images, see "Relation" note).

title_tesim: James C. Flood Mansion, San Fransisco, 1930s
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2946. San Francisco. Nob Hill, Bay Bridge, Palo Alto (Stanford), Carmel.
named_subject_tesim: James C. Flood Mansion (San Francisco, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the James C. Flood Mansion (Pacific-Union Club). The Neoclassical style brownstone mansion is located at 1000 California Street, situated atop the tree-lined streets of Nob Hill. 1920s and 30s era cars line both sides of the street. Seen farther down the street, at right, is the historic Fairmont Hotel.

The James C. Flood Mansion is the home of the Pacific-Union Club, a private social club founded in 1889. The mansion was originally built for Flood in 1886. Both the Flood mansion and the neighboring Fairmont hotel were the only two buildings on Nob Hill to structurally survive the earthquake and fire of 1906. The mansion is listed as a U.S. National Historic Landmark, and as a San Francisco Designated Landmark (no. 64). It is also listed on the U.S. National Register of Historic Places. Willis Polk and Augustus Laver, architects.
combined_subject_ssim: James C. Flood Mansion (San Francisco, Calif.)

description_tesim: The hotel, at 999 California St., was built in 1925 by the architectural firm, Weeks and Day. Charles Peter Weeks (1870-1928) was the designer, William Peyton Day (1886-1966) the engineer.
Photograph of the James C. Flood Mansion (left) and the Intercontinental Mark Hopkins hotel, on Nob Hill.

The James C. Flood Mansion, designed by Augustus Laver and Willis Polk, and the Fairmont Hotel were the only buildings to structurally survive the 1906 earthquake. The mansion now houses the Pacific-Union Club and is located at 1000 California St.

Panorama from the Bay Cities Guaranty Building facing northwest towards buildings between 2nd Street (left) and 3rd-4th Streets (with an alley in between). The back of the Mar Vista Hotel and Apartments building is visible (left foreground, 2nd St. and Arizona Ave.). Three historic apartment buildings are visible, L to R: the Sovereign Apartment Hotel is the large white building visible along 2nd St. in the background on the left (2nd St. and Washington Ave.), the Embassy Hotel Apartments building is in the center (3rd St. and Washington Ave.), and the Charmont Apartments building is the large white edifice on the right beyond the middle ground (4th and California Ave.). The Santa Monica mountain range is in the distance.
combined_subject_ssim: Mar Vista Hotel and Apartments (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Leo Carrillo riding a rearing Palomino horse in the California Admission Day Parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
named_subject_tesim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Leo Carrillo riding a rearing Palomino horse in the California Admission Day Parade. Carrillo (wearing his characteristic wide-brimmed cowboy hat, with cowboy boots and a gun belt) looks toward the camera with a slight smile as his horse lifts its forelegs off the ground. They perform in front of spectators lining the parade route along 4th St. near Santa Monica Blvd. The Security First National Bank building stands in the background.
Carrillo is photographed on the first half of the parade route along 4th Street. A second photo shows him on the second half of the route along Ocean Ave. (image ark no. 21198/zz002d7fpt).

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.

The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.
combined_subject_ssim: Native Daughters of the Golden West
Native Sons of the Golden West

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The parade (held September 9th) was noted in the article, "California Natives will Parade Today: Thirty-five Hundred to Join March in Admission Day Celebration," Los Angeles Times, 9 Sep. 1937: A1.

Participants in the California Admission Day celebration and parade, photographed on the steps of City Hall. Identified participants include: Postmaster Philip T. Hill (far left), Mayor Edmond Gillette (seen at center wearing a cowboy hat, boots, a sheriff’s badge, and a gun belt with two guns); columnist Irvin S. Cobb stands to his right. The young boy standing in front of Cobb wears a cowboy hat, neckerchief, wrist cuffs, and cowhide chaps.
The child is photographed in two additional images (see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7f6k and 21198/zz002d7ff6).

description_tesim: Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

Photograph of a cargo truck carrying a military brass band during the Santa Monica Armistice Day parade. Two soldiers in view hold their instruments beside them while the tuba player plays. Printed on the passenger-side of the truck: "63rd CA. Btry. A." Printed on the door is the Unit's Coat of Arms - a shield with three winged projectiles, one and two counter changed, above a scroll reading, "Amor Patriae" ("Love of Country"). Printed on the hood: "W-4772." In addition, a plate riveted to the truck's front grill reads: "Pvt. Chambles;" a plate attached to the bumper reads "63rd CA A-21." In the background are cars parked along the street near a two-story building.

title_tesim: Flag bearers marching in the Armistice Day parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2876. Armistice Day 1938, Santa Monica, also Soldiers Home. Col. RA Bringham Mgr, John F. Eaton, Veteran (age 92) of John A Martin Post GAR. Parade of Santa Monica Streets.

description_tesim: Photograph of veterans serving as color guards marching in the Santa Monica Armistice Day parade. At foreground are six men in uniform (one partially in view at left). They carry bayonets on their right shoulders and wear a variety of uniforms - some reminiscent of Spanish-American War regalia, including campaign hats, canvas leggings and cartridge belts around their waists. Two at left wear garrison caps with emblems and (possibly) the names of their posts embroidered on the sides. also wear "Sam Browne" belts - a wide belt with a narrow strap across the chest. These two men are possibly American Legionnaires. The soldiers are followed by a number of men and women bearing the American flag.

Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

Photograph of a Marine drill team marching in the Santa Monica Armistice Day Parade. The men wear parade dress uniforms - including visor hats, jackets with standing collars and white band belts. They carry rifles over their right shoulders. They are followed by a Navy drill team, also viewed with rifles over the shoulder. They wear the characteristic sailor hat, scarf at neck, cartridge belts, and spats (leggings). Spectators are viewed on either side of the procession.

description_tesim: The tin toy, by Marx, is "Tidy Tim," identified by the inscription on trash bin, which also includes the inscriptions "D.S.C" and "Keep your City Spick & Span Says Tidy Tim the Clean-up Man" (seen online). Online examples of this toy are dated from 1933 into the 1950's.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

Photograph of two boys seated outdoors on a step in front of French doors playing with a "Tidy Tim" tin wind-up toy featuring a trash collector pushing a trash bin on wheels. Photographed at the Children's Home Society.

The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.

description_tesim: Photograph of Canadian Legionnaires  marching in the seventh annual California State convention of the Canadian Legion, British Empire Service League. The men heading up the line wear light-colored pants, suit jackets and ties with garrison caps. Several wear medals on their chests. They are followed by a Scottish bagpipe band (wearing traditional Scottish highland attire) and flag bearers carrying Union, American, and Legion flags. The procession follows street car tracks at about 314 Santa Monica Boulevard near Third Street, with the Junipher building seen at right. The building housed an S.H. Kress & Co. 5-10-25 Cent Store, a Union Pacific ticket office, a jeweler and a See's Candy store amongst other businesses. The tall edifice standing farther down the street is the Guaranty Trust Bank Building (221-225 Santa Monica Blvd.).

The event was covered in a Los Times article, "Legionnaire Conclave Climaxed by Parade: Canadian Veterans March 1500 Strong Through Streets of Santa Monica," 6 Jul. 1937: A17. Also, the Bartlett collection includes related clippings from a Santa Monica newspaper. The 2-page article is titled, "Legion Parade Today Feature of Convention," 5 Jul. 1937. (see "Relation" note).

description_tesim: Photograph of a Ladies' Auxiliary group, marching in the seventh annual California State convention of the Canadian Legion, British Empire Service League. The women are lead by an Auxiliary Commander wearing a white jacket and skirt with garrison cap. She is followed by flag bearers carrying the Union Jack, American and Service Legion flags. The procession follows street car tracks along Santa Monica Boulevard in the vicinity of Fourth Street. Spectators view the parade in front of businesses whose visible signs read: "Green...Café," "Jack Posner Credit Jeweler," and "California Bank."

The event was covered in a Los Times article, "Legionnaire Conclave Climaxed by Parade: Canadian Veterans March 1500 Strong Through Streets of Santa Monica," 6 Jul. 1937: A17. Also, the Bartlett collection includes related clippings from a Santa Monica newspaper. The 2-page article is titled, "Legion Parade Today Feature of Convention," 5 Jul. 1937. (see "Relation" note).

title_tesim: O. H. Hewlett with 7 women in 19th-century pioneer dress at the Pioneer Day event, Ocean Park, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2855. 7/20/38. O. H. Hewlett. Utah Society. Mormon people.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Group portrait of 7 women, probably members of the California Daughters of Pioneers, dressed in mid-19th century pioneer dress, standing behind the General Chairman, O. H. Hewlett, seated on the grass.

Pioneer Day is an official holiday celebrated in the U.S. state of Utah, with some celebrations in regions of surrounding states originally settled by Mormon pioneers. In 1938, Pioneer Day was celebrated on July 23 in Ocean Park, a neighborhood of Santa Monica. It was organized under the auspices of the society, California Daughters of Pioneers, and the presidents of five Southland Mormon stakes, and directed by General Chairman O. H. Hewlett. The day was spent in speeches, picnicking and reunion.

description_tesim: The "Pacifica" statue was 80 ft. (24 m) tall and was placed in front of a "prayer curtain" formed of star-shaped steel bangles that rippled in the wind. Vivid orange and blue lights washed the curtain at night.

Night view of the "Pacifica" statue by Ralph Stackpole in the Court of Pacifica by architect Timothy Pflueger at the Golden Gate International Exposition. The Fountain of Western Waters emanates from the base of the statue, running down a stepped cascade into a central pool; several statues are placed around the pool.

description_tesim: The Hotel San Diego was developed by John D. Spreckles, designed by architect Harrison Albright in the "Chicago style" and constructed in 1914, in anticipation of the Panama California International Exposition in 1915-1916. It was located at 339 West Broadway and was designated as San Diego Historical Site No. 175 in 1983. It was demolished in April 2006.

title_tesim: Central Tower Building on Fourth Street, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text on negative sleeve: Santa Monica, Downtown Streets, 1938
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Central Tower Building was designed by M. Eugene Durfee and constructed in 1929 by the contractors J. Westley Forderer (west wing and tower) and Claude L. Freeman (north wing). It is an example of the Art Deco style, with vertical pilasters; recessed spandrels; stylized parapets on the second, sixth, seventh and eighth floors; and decorative patterns including the zig-zag styled ornamentation above the commercial storefronts. It was constructed of reinforced concrete with brick infill and finished with stucco. The form consists of two-story store front wings flanking an eight-story tower. In this photograph the original metal screening on the exterior of the storefront transom windows, the black-tiled store front base and black and white storefront checkered flooring are visible.

Elevated view of the Central Tower Building from the intersection on Fourth Street and Broadway. The  commercial building has a row of shops/stores lined up along the ground level, including "Baida's Persian Chinese Rugs", "Tower Cafe," "Clark Furniture Co." In the foreground is a lit sign for "Southern California Edison" while the "Bay Cities Building" (now part of the Third Street Promenade) stands tall in the background.

Also in the picture are several stores and signs reading, "Lido Cafe, open all night," "Hotel Apts Lido," "Railway Express Agency," "1451 Mabry Bedding & Rugs," "Singer Sewing Machine." In the background is a large sign for "H. C. Henshey Co." department Store. In the foreground to the right is a sign for "Standard Stations Inc. The Stop Sign for complete se[rvic]e. America['s fa]vorite Service Sta[tion] System."

title_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall, Santa Monica, 1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, New City Hall Building itself. 12/1/39. "Dedication" [struck out].
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a view of the historic Santa Monica City Hall looking north-east from the lawns leading up to the building. The words "City Hall" appear over the grand entrance which is lined with geometric patterned tiles. The American flag flutters in the wind against the sky. The building itself is considered one of the best examples of PWA (Public Works Administration) Moderne architecture, a combination of the Art deco and Art moderne characteristics. This style was popularised by depression-era architects, found mostly in buildings financed by the federal government.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Entrance to Santa Monica city hall, Santa Monica, 1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, New City Hall Building itself. 12/1/39. "Dedication" [struck out].
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: View of the entrance to the historic Santa Monica city hall. A plaque to the left of the entrance [right of the photograph] reads, "Santa Monica City Hall... E. S. Gillette... T. D. Plumer... W. W. Milliken... 1938". Three young men stand at the entrance. The entrance is outlined by geometric patterned tiles that reflect Art deco characteristics. The tiles were crafted by the California-based Gladding, McBean Tile Company, a renowned company that shaped the modern ceramics industry. The city hall is itself said to be one of the best examples of PWA (Public Works Administration) Moderne architecture, a combination of Art deco and Art moderne characteristics. This style was popularized by depression-era architects, found mostly in buildings financed by the federal government.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story "Ogwalla Springs," by Adelbert Bartlett and Mrs. Williams, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761, "Ogwallah Springs", desert story illustrations, 9/1/42. By Mrs. Williams & Adelbert Bartlett.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story, "Ogwalla Springs," written by Adelbert Bartlett, possibly drawn by Mrs. Williams (no full name or initials on illustration). A diagonal layout carries across both pages, with illustrations rendered on the lower right corners of both pages, while the upper left areas are left open for printed text. Illustrations on left-hand page include two horse and riders (at least one is female), cacti, and a sun with human face (seen winking at the viewer). A decorative border separates this scene from the scene at left. Here, a glamorous woman sits in bed with a tray above her lap. A tea pot, cup and saucer, plate and napkin are depicted. Shelves displaying several pairs of shoes are drawn at left. Text above her reads: "Breakfast in Bed Club." The name "Dolle," a drawing of an arrow shooting through two hearts, and the number "6" (likely page number) are placed at the bottom of the page.
Related image includes caption: "illustrations by W. Williams" (image ark no. 21198/zz002d89nt).

title_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story "Ogwalla Springs," by Adelbert Bartlett and Mrs. Williams, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761, "Ogwallah Springs", desert story illustrations, 9/1/42. By Mrs. Williams & Adelbert Bartlett.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Illustrated pages from the story, "Ogwalla Springs," written by Adelbert Bartlett, possibly drawn by Mrs. Williams (no full name or initials on illustration). Page at left depicts two views of the road entering and leaving "Ogwalla." Text on sign hanging from a post at top reads: "Welcome to Ogwalla." [Text in parenthesis:] "at or near beginning." Text on sign hanging from a post at bottom reads: "Ogwalla Bids You Come Again." [Text in parenthesis:] "at or near end." Illustration at right depicts a woman wearing a cowboy hat, boots, a neckerchief and star studded wrist band, holding a gun and shooting at a target. Text above her reads: "Take this! and this!! and this!!" A Native American man wearing a feathered head piece and beads around his neck peeps into view at right. Space is left for additional text, followed by a rope border and the number "3" - likely indicating the page number.
Related image includes caption: "illustrations by W. Williams" (image ark no. 21198/zz002d89nt).

description_tesim: Photograph of an illustrated page from the story, "Ogwalla Springs," written by Adelbert Bartlett, possibly drawn by Mrs. Williams (no full name or initials on illustration). The drawing depicts an oasis of sorts, with two figures relaxing and swimming in what appears to be a body of water set within a desert (rendered in short hand with a lone palm tree and an animal's skull seen at foreground). A dwelling sits at right, near farm animals (possibly mules) and a buggy. Short-hand birds fly above, while the sun shines over the hills rendered in the background. Text at top reads: "(double page)...Ogwallah."
Related image includes caption: "illustrations by W. Williams" (image ark no. 21198/zz002d89nt).

title_tesim: Illustrated pages from a story, "Recipe for Charm," by Adelbert Bartlett, illustrated by W. Williams, 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2761, "Ogwallah Springs", desert story illustrations, 9/1/42. By Mrs. Williams & Adelbert Bartlett.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Two pen and ink illustrations photographed side-by-side. Drawing on the left features a Native American seated cross-legged. He eats the contents of a bowl held in his lap. At right sits a second bowl with Southwestern designs, and pipe. A mesa stands in the background. The illustration at right features a man seated on a rock, smoking a pipe, chin resting on a clenched fist. He sits across from a squirrel. Text in speech bubble above him reads: "a nut to crack... Yes - that's the problem." Beyond the scene, a path leads toward a dwelling with smoke escaping from its chimney while the sun sets in the distance. Caption on rock at foreground: "Illustrations by W. Williams."

title_tesim: Art room open house night, Santa Monica, 1937-1939
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SMHi Art Rm. Open House Nite. [Sleeve is an envelope addressed to Mr. Adelbert Bartlett with postal cancelation: "Los Angeles, Calif., Apr 25, 1937."]
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photographs housed with this negative sleeve are related to others from a sleeve labeled "Carolyn's negatives." The photographs are visually and thematically similar. In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of Santa Monica High School structures built between 1937 and 1939 by the Works Project Administration (WPA). Both sleeves also included construction site images, likely related to the WPA’s reconstruction project (image ark nos. 21198/zz002d8j8h and 198/zz002d8k9h).

Photograph of students in an art classroom at Santa Monica High School. Several students work on their canvases while a few direct their gazes toward the camera. Two students (one obscured) work on a Spanish-themed mural that is stretched across a blackboard. One of these students is a young woman, seen with paint brush in hand, turned with her gaze toward an older woman wearing a wide brimmed hat. She looks toward the camera. The students (and parent?) were photographed by Carolyn Bartlett.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica High School (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Fancy Dress participants at the Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade, Santa Monica, 1936
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2927, Ocean Park, Children's Parade, 1936
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The event was covered in the Los  article, "Hundreds of Children to be in Floral Parade,"  Los Angeles Times , 27 Aug. 1936: 10.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by Santa Monica club women and civic leaders, with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

Photograph of children participating in the fancy dress competition at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. The children wear a variety of dresses and hats - including one young woman wearing a Prairie-style dress and bonnet. Twin girls (standing at opposite ends on the front row), each holding a bunch of poppies, wear identical short dresses and hats - their hair also  festooned with poppies. Amidst the "old-fashioned" dresses, one girl (standing front and center) wears a costume evoking a Persian dancer. Behind her, a young woman (wearing a top hat) holds an accordion with "Shirley" written down the length of the instrument. Participant numbers are visible on two of the girls' arms.

title_tesim: Girl dressed as Scarlett O'Hara at the Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade, Santa Monica, 1936
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2927, Ocean Park, Children's Parade, 1936

Photograph of a girl dressed as Scarlett O'Hara at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. The girl is viewed riding a float named after the famous character. The girl wears a multi-tiered dress with ruffled bodice and puff sleeves, and holds an open parasol. Her float is comprised of two parts: a roof structure covered in vines and supported by four columns, and a flat bed lined with flowers and fern leaves. Two picket fences entwined with vines and an American flag (seen at right) complete the design.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by Santa Monica club women and civic leaders, with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

Photograph of the "Leonie Lester Show Boat," featured in the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. The float (entry number 191) carries one young girl wearing a showgirl costume (a tiered skirt, midriff-baring top and lace jacket, and headband with feathers), and is flanked by costumed children - several of whom appear to be pulling the float with ropes.

title_tesim: Jean Moorhead posing on a "surfboard," Santa Monica, 1951
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Jean Moorhead model. 20th Century Fox, Elks 906, 1951. Composite Elks convention.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Jean Moorhead, wearing a stripped bathing suit, posing as if riding a surfboard. Moorhead is actually standing on a ironing board situated on a lawn with foliage, flowering shrubs, and a chain link fence seen in the background. A portion of an open newspaper is visible in the foreground.
Barbara Jean Moorhead was an actress and model. She was Playboy Playmate of the Month, October 1955 (as Jean Moorehead).

Photograph used in a composite image depicting Moorhead riding the waves along the Santa Monica Coast, with the Breakers, Edgewater, and Hotel Casa Del Mar in the background (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8tgg).
